title: WSET Aussie Diploma graduates recognised with prestigious awards
summary:
WSET celebrated the outstanding achievements of its latest cohort of Level 4 Diploma in Wines graduates at its annual graduation ceremony and prize reception at the QEII Centre in Westminster, London. A total of 714 new Diploma graduates from 2024/25 were honoured at the ceremony representing 45 countries across six continents. They join a global community of over 14,500 WSET Diploma alumni “shaping the future of the drinks industry worldwide”. Among this year's top students, Australian graduates claimed two of the most prestigious prizes: Sebastian Iannuzzi of Sydney Wine Academy took the Vintners' Cup – awarded to the highest overall performer – and Anthony Justice, also of Sydney Wine Academy, claimed the Penfolds Asia Pacific Scholarship – awarded to the top performing graduate in Asia-Pacific.

The post WSET Aussie Diploma graduates recognised with prestigious awards appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
LTR: Michelle Brampton, Sebastian Iannuzzi, Anthony Justice and Helena Edgerton. Image supplied

WSET celebrated the outstanding achievements of its latest cohort of Level 4 Diploma in Wines graduates at its annual graduation ceremony and prize reception at the QEII Centre in Westminster, London.

A total of 714 new Diploma graduates from 2024/25 were honoured at the ceremony representing 45 countries across six continents. They join a global community of over 14,500 WSET Diploma alumni “shaping the future of the drinks industry worldwide”.

The ceremony was hosted by WSET CEO Michelle Brampton, who was joined on stage by WSET’s honorary president Dr Laura Catena, managing director of Catena Zapata.

Brampton said the organisation is incredibly proud to celebrate with the class of 2024/25.

“The WSET Diploma asks a great deal of students, and this year’s graduates have risen to the challenge. Our Diploma alumni are a vibrant global community, shaping the future of the drinks industry in so many ways.

“I’d also like to personally congratulate our scholarship and prize winners, whose exceptional results, together with the stories they’ve shared about the impact WSET has had on their lives and careers, continue to inspire us all,” she said.

Catena added that graduation marks an important moment in a lifelong commitment to learning.

“The intellectual curiosity and discipline shown by this year’s graduates give me great confidence in the future of our industry.”

Among this year’s top students, Australian graduates claimed two of the most prestigious prizes: Sebastian Iannuzzi of Sydney Wine Academy took the Vintners’ Cup – awarded to the highest overall performer – and Anthony Justice, also of Sydney Wine Academy, claimed the Penfolds Asia Pacific Scholarship – awarded to the top performing graduate in Asia-Pacific.

The Vintners’ Cup, sponsored by The Worshipful Company of Vintners since 2004, is awarded annually to the graduate achieving the highest aggregate mark across all units of the WSET Level 4 Diploma in Wines.

This year’s winner Iannuzzi is production manager at Mount Eyre Vineyards and studied at the Sydney Wine Academy. His passion for wine began at his family’s Hunter Valley vineyard, where childhood weekends immersed him in viticulture and winemaking.

“Winning the Vintners’ Cup is an immense honour. My disbelief led me to reread the email several times. The further I progressed through the Diploma, the more acutely aware I became of how much I still had to learn. Surrounded by industry professionals, knowledgeable educators and exceptionally capable classmates, it’s easy to feel unlearned. The Vintners’ Cup served as a powerful reminder of how much I had achieved,” he said.

Justice, who was awarded the Penfolds Asia Pacific Scholarship, said he is delighted to have been awarded the scholarship.

“Previous study trips have been incredibly useful in gaining a much better understanding of various aspects of the industry and I am excited to put this scholarship to good use,” he said.

title: Clare Valley provides update on 2026 ‘season of resilience’ vintage
summary:
The Clare Valley Wine & Grape Association (CVWGA) is pleased to share a “confident outlook” on the 2026 vintage. CVWGA claims that early indicators are pointing to wines of “purity, balance and regional character, particularly for the Valley’s celebrated Riesling”. It claimed that the growing season presented a few challenges, with variable conditions requiring careful vineyard management and considered decision-making as harvest approached. CVWGA chair Olivia Hoffmann Barry said that the community has navigated a complex season with skill and patience. “The fruit that was harvested is showing excellent quality, with bright, well-defined flavours and a clear sense of regional character,” she said.

The post Clare Valley provides update on 2026 ‘season of resilience’ vintage appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Clare Valley Wine & Grape Association (CVWGA) is pleased to share a “confident outlook” on the 2026 vintage.

CVWGA claims that early indicators are pointing to wines of “purity, balance and regional character, particularly for the Valley’s celebrated Riesling”.

It claimed that the growing season presented a few challenges, with variable conditions requiring careful vineyard management and considered decision-making as harvest approached.

“A cool start to the season and welcome spring rainfall supported strong canopy health, while intermittent rainfall through March brought periods of pressure, placing a premium on timing, site selection and attention to detail in the vineyard.

“In well-managed sites, fruit condition remained sound, with only low levels of disease observed. In some cases, growers made disciplined choices not to harvest all fruit, ensuring that quality remained the defining priority,” CVWGA said.

CVWGA chair Olivia Hoffmann Barry said that the community has navigated a complex season with skill and patience.

“The fruit that was harvested is showing excellent quality, with bright, well-defined flavours and a clear sense of regional character,” she said.

Across the Clare Valley, early tastings and ferments are revealing “wines of clarity and precision” which CVWGA said were shaped as much by the season as by the decisions made in response to it.

Variation across subregions and picking windows is also expected to deliver a “diverse and expressive vintage, reflecting the individuality of sites and styles”.

The organisation claims that the region’s hallmark variety, Riesling, is already emerging as a standout with “vibrant citrus expression, lifted aromatics and fine, persistent acidity”.

Yields of high-quality Riesling have remained solid, CVWGA claimed, with good availability to meet the “growing global demand for the variety”.

Michael Kane of Knappstein Wines said that there is no “perfect growing season”.

“It’s our role as growers and winemakers to respond to those conditions and capture the best of what the vineyard offers. In 2026, that has resulted in wines of exceptional quality, with fine Rieslings and classic, medium-bodied reds that speak clearly of Clare Valley,” he said.

“Beyond Riesling, other white varieties such as Fiano are showing strong results, reflecting the region’s evolving varietal landscape.

“Cabernet Sauvignon is a particular highlight among the reds in the region, with dry conditions up to veraison leading to smaller berries. This,coupled with an extended ripening period (hang time) resulted in wines with intense varietal expression, depth and structure.

“Shiraz and Grenache are also showing excellent promise, contributing to a vintage defined by refined elegance and superb balance between fruit and tannin elements,” CVWGA said.

title: ASVO concludes search for new executive director
summary:
Following an “extended search for the ideal candidate”, the Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology (ASVO) has announced that Emily White starts today as its new executive director. ASVO said White brings “substantial experience in the wine and agribusiness industries” along with a “strong background of demonstrated skill in marketing and business development”. White shared that she is excited to be starting the role and is honoured to lead “an organisation with longstanding equity in the Australian wine community”. “ASVO plays a vital role in bringing together technical leadership and industry - from publishing research that drives practical improvements, to creating opportunities for members to connect and share knowledge across oenology and viticulture”.

The post ASVO concludes search for new executive director appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Emily White, the new executive director for the ASVO. Image supplied

Following an “extended search for the ideal candidate”, the Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology (ASVO) has announced that Emily White starts today as its new executive director.

ASVO said White brings “substantial experience in the wine and agribusiness industries” along with a “strong background of demonstrated skill in marketing and business development”.

White shared that she is excited to be starting the role and is honoured to lead “an organisation with longstanding equity in the Australian wine community”.

“ASVO plays a vital role in bringing together technical leadership and industry – from publishing research that drives practical improvements, to creating opportunities for members to connect and share knowledge across oenology and viticulture.

“I am particularly committed to strengthening partnerships and collaborative platforms that deliver real value to our members, while building meaningful connections across research, industry, and education. My focus will be on increasing membership value and leveraging ASVO’s trusted position to benefit both current and future generations shaping Australia’s wine sector,” she said.

Samantha Scarratt, president of ASVO, said that the organisation is delighted to have found “such a strong candidate to take the ASVO forward”.

“Emily’s strength in marketing and business development will assist the ASVO to grow its membership base and profile and will be an excellent complement to the extensive viticulture and winemaking – and recently added editorial and financial – expertise of the ASVO board of directors. Emily brings a different skillset to the ASVO, which will help lead the organisation into the future,” Scarratt said.

From today, White will collaborate closely with retiring executive director Chris Waters for the next two months as she readies herself to take over the management of ASVO services and activities.

She holds a Bachelor of Viticultural Science degree and has further studied wine marketing. ASVO said that White has a “distinguished track-record” in senior marketing and has led global as well as regional initiatives across agricultural sales, marketing, strategy and industry engagement roles.

White’s experience includes leading teams across Taltarni/Clover Hill Vineyards, Treasury Wine Estates and Accolade Wines, where she has “consistently driven brand growth, market expansion and commercial partnerships in highly competitive markets”.

title: Barossa Valley is the most affordable wine region in the world, according to new report
summary:
According to Knight Frank’s 2026 Wealth Report, the Barossa Valley is one of the most affordable wine regions by hectare globally. The firm’s global vineyard index found US $1 million can buy 18.18 hectares of land in the region, the greatest amount of land among the regions examined. The cost of land in the Barossa Valley was US $55,000 per hectare at the end of 2025 following a 10% reduction over the year, making it the most affordable wine region in the world. Stellenbosch in South Africa was the next most affordable at US $60,000 per hectare, while Italy's Piedmont (Barolo) was the most expensive at US $2.7 million per hectare.

The post Barossa Valley is the most affordable wine region in the world, according to new report appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
According to Knight Frank’s 2026 Wealth Report, the Barossa Valley is the most affordable wine region by hectare globally, despite its reputation as one of Australia’s premium wine regions.

The firm’s global vineyard index found US $1 million can buy 18.18 hectares of land in the region, the greatest amount of land among the regions examined.

The cost of land in the Barossa Valley was US $55,000 per hectare at the end of 2025 following a 10% reduction over the year, making it the most affordable wine region in the world.

Stellenbosch in South Africa was the next most affordable at US $60,000 per hectare, while Italy’s Piedmont (Barolo) was the most expensive of regions included in the index at US $2.7 million per hectare.

According to the report, despite persistent headwinds, the vineyard market is “far from being in decline”. However, it claims there are “forces reshaping the global wine production map” and “creating exciting opportunities for forward-looking winemakers and investors”.

Knight Frank Australia partner, agribusiness Jason Oster said the Barossa Valley’s recognition in the report as a wine region to watch “reaffirmed its reputation as one of the world’s great wine-producing areas”.

“With more than 175 years of winemaking history and home to some of the oldest producing vines in the world, the Barossa Valley continues to attract interest from high-net-worth-individuals seeking a combination of agricultural investment, lifestyle appeal and long-term capital preservation.

“The Barossa Valley offers a great value proposition with large vineyard scale for comparatively low capital outlay, and strong growth prospects, especially for those that are looking to make the most of the premiumisation trend.

“Australia’s winemakers continue to struggle with oversupply and the shift in consumer tastes towards lighter wines. But for those who are pursuing premiumisation strategies, focusing on higher quality regional wines, like Barossa Valley, and offering consumers unique provenance and storytelling, often through cellar-door sales, will thrive and weather the storm.

“Although there has been some downward pressure on vineyard returns, there is optimism within the market for those with premium product,” Oster said.

The research identifies five trends impacting the global wine industry, with the Barossa Valley and nearby Eden Valley named as regions to watch in Australia for the ‘trading up’ trend.

The report emphasised that “while overall global consumption of wine is falling, values are rising, with drinkers increasingly choosing higher-quality, artisanal and terroir-specific wines, rather than mass-market bottles”.

“Those vineyards with a strong narrative story focusing on provenance and craftsmanship are best placed to differentiate themselves,” the report said.

title: Barossa boutique winery wins Shiraz Producer of the Year at 2026 London Wine Competition
summary:
Family-owned Barossa Valley boutique winery Kellermeister Wines has been awarded Shiraz Producer of the Year at the global 2026 London Wine Competition. All three of Kellermeister’s flagship Shiraz wines were awarded Gold medals, with it’s the Meister Shiraz receiving a Double Gold. Winemaker Mark Pearce said it is a joy to work with his sons Hugo and Rupert, who have joined him in working at the winery for a few years now. “Together we continue to chase the dream of crafting the very best wines we can produce in this beautiful region, which I believe is the archetypal home for Shiraz in the New World - informed by the timeless wisdom of the inspiration I derive from the Rhône in France, and the unique power of our ancient vines in the Barossa,” he said.

The post Barossa boutique winery wins Shiraz Producer of the Year at 2026 London Wine Competition appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Winemaker Mark Pearce with his sons Hugo and Rupert. Image supplied

Family-owned Barossa Valley boutique winery Kellermeister Wines has been awarded Shiraz Producer of the Year at the global 2026 London Wine Competition.

All three of Kellermeister’s flagship Shiraz wines were awarded Gold medals, with it’s the Meister Shiraz receiving a Double Gold.

Winemaker Mark Pearce said it is a joy to work with his sons Hugo and Rupert, who have joined him in working at the winery for a few years now.

“Together we continue to chase the dream of crafting the very best wines we can produce in this beautiful region, which I believe is the archetypal home for Shiraz in the New World – informed by the timeless wisdom of the inspiration I derive from the Rhône in France, and the unique power of our ancient vines in the Barossa,” he said.

The London Wine Competition “is renowned for its rigorous blind-tas1ng format, judged by more than 70 of the world’s most respected wine professionals, including Masters of Wine, Master Sommeliers, senior buyers, and wine directors”.

Since Pearce joined the winery in 2010 and acquired it in 2012, the estate said it has amassed one of the most consistent award records for its Shiraz wines in the world, including Best Shiraz in the World (International Wine Competition 2012, Syrah du Monde 2019), Wine of the Year at the London Wine Competition 2019 (plus Best Wine by Country [Australia], Best Wine by Varietal [Shiraz], and Best Wine by Quality), World’s Top 5 Shiraz (Syrah du Monde 2021), World’s Top 10 Shiraz (Syrah du Monde 2023), and back-to-back Trophy Best Barossa Shiraz titles at the London International Wine Competition in 2023 and 2024.

Kellermeister’s The Meister retails for $350 per bottle, while its Wild Witch and Black Sash Shiraz retail for $88 and $92 per bottle.

title: New data shows Champagne shipments to Australia increased in 2025
summary:
Comité Champagne has revealed that more than 7.5 million bottles of Champagne were shipped to Australia in 2025, an increase of 3.5% volume from the previous year. With these figures, Australia remains the seventh largest global market for Champagne in the world. Total shipments of Champagne worldwide for 2025 was 266 million bottles, down 2% from the 271.4 million bottles distributed in 2024. International markets accounted for nearly 152 million of those bottles, whilst the French market represented 114 million bottles. “With a strong appellation and coordinated actions, we will continue to bring joy to all those around the world who love fine wines, dreams, fraternity, celebration and life,” said David Chatillon, president of the Union des Maisons de Champagne and co-president of Comité Champagne.

The post New data shows Champagne shipments to Australia increased in 2025 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Comité Champagne has revealed that more than 7.5 million bottles of Champagne were shipped to Australia in 2025, an increase of 3.5% volume from the previous year.

With these figures, Australia remains the seventh largest global market for Champagne in the world.

Total shipments of Champagne worldwide for 2025 was 266 million bottles, down 2% from the 271.4 million bottles distributed in 2024. International markets accounted for nearly 152 million of those bottles, whilst the French market represented 114 million bottles.

“With a strong appellation and coordinated actions, we will continue to bring joy to all those around the world who love fine wines, dreams, fraternity, celebration and life,” said David Chatillon, president of the Union des Maisons de Champagne and co-president of Comité Champagne.

Maxime Toubart, president of the Syndicat Général des Vignerons and co-president of the Comité Champagne, said that the French market is a showcase of its appellation that must be strengthened.

“We have all the assets we need – excellent wines, unique expertise and collective strength – to consolidate this position and promote Champagne,” Toubart said.

“The Australian market has remained resilient for Champagne, despite ongoing economic pressures and global geopolitical uncertainty,” added John Noble, director of the Champagne Bureau Australia.

“The versatility and diversity of Champagne styles ensure the wines resonate with the Australian lifestyle and across a wide range of occasions – from relaxed, everyday dining to the celebration of life’s most significant moments.”

The top ten export markets for Champagne by volume are as follows:

  1. United States of America: 26 462 000
  2. United Kingdom: 22 728 000
  3. Japan: 13 283 000
  4. Germany: 9 616 000
  5. Belgium: 7 823 000
  6. Italy: 7 785 000
  7. Australia: 7 555 000
  8. Switzerland: 5 069 000
  9. Spain: 4 004 000
  10. Canada: 2 893 000

title: Final week to have your say on the Murray-Darling Basin
summary:
The Murray-Darling Basin Authority (MDBA) has advised that this is the final week for members of the community to have their say on the future of the Murray-Darling Basin. The MDBA encourages communities to make a submission before the deadline on 1 May at 5pm. The organisation said it has already received more than 600 submissions, reflecting a “strong interest in how the Basin’s water resources should be managed over the next decade”.

The post Final week to have your say on the Murray-Darling Basin appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Murray-Darling Basin Authority (MDBA) has advised that this is the final week for members of the community to have their say on the future of the Murray-Darling Basin.

The MDBA encourages communities to make a submission before the deadline on 1 May at 5pm.

The organisation said it has already received more than 600 submissions, reflecting a “strong interest in how the Basin’s water resources should be managed over the next decade”.

MDBA chief executive Andrew McConville said the review is a once-in-a-decade chance to influence how water is shared, rivers are protected and communities are supported across the Basin.

“This review brings together the latest science, climate data and lived experience from across the Basin to assess what is working, what needs to change and how the plan must evolve.

“Our discussion paper sets out some options. They are starting points for discussion, not predetermined decisions. Competing viewpoints are not a weakness of the Basin reform process; they are its strength. Hearing those perspectives is integral to refining the plan.

“The Basin Plan was based on the best evidence available at the time. A decade on, we have more data, more experience and a changing climate, so it’s the right time to reassess how we manage our waterways and environment,” he explained.

Since consultation opened in February, the MDBA said it has engaged with more than 2,700 participants through over 200 interactions across Basin communities.

McConville claimed that the MDBA has taken a “very deliberate approach to consultation”.

“We know people are more comfortable sharing their views in smaller, local conversations rather than large town halls. That’s why our teams have been on the ground across the Basin, meeting people where they are.

“We’re hearing a wide range of views across the Basin, and that’s exactly what we need. People are challenging us, raising new ideas and testing our thinking and we welcome that. Good policy comes from honest conversations, not everyone agreeing.

“We’ll keep engaging right up until submissions close next week. Every voice matters and we want to ensure people across the Basin have the opportunity to contribute.”

The authority said that submissions will inform recommendations to Basin Governments on how the Basin Plan can be improved to respond to emerging challenges, including climate variability and changing water needs.

Submissions information can be found here. Community members who would like assistance submitting can also call the MDBA consultation hotline on 1800 230 067.

The MDBA said it has already visited 91 towns and held webinars, roundtables, and a Basin-wide Summit to gather perspectives.

The post Final week to have your say on the Murray-Darling Basin appeared first on Winetitles.

title: New Zealand Winegrowers welcomes support for the India Free Trade Agreement
summary:
New Zealand Winegrowers has welcomed the announcement of Labour’s support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement between New Zealand and India, “reinforcing trade as a shared priority”. Philip Gregan, CEO of New Zealand Winegrowers, said maintenance of bipartisan alignment on trade is very positive as it gives exporters certainty and reinforces New Zealand’s reputation as a long-term trading partner. “India represents a significant long-term opportunity for New Zealand wine, and sustained cross-party commitment will be key to delivering a high-quality agreement with real commercial outcomes,” he said.

The post New Zealand Winegrowers welcomes support for the India Free Trade Agreement appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
New Zealand Winegrowers has shown support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement with India. Image courtesy New Zealand Winegrowers

New Zealand Winegrowers has welcomed the announcement of Labour’s support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement between New Zealand and India, “reinforcing trade as a shared priority”.

Philip Gregan, CEO of New Zealand Winegrowers, said maintenance of bipartisan alignment on trade is very positive as it gives exporters certainty and reinforces New Zealand’s reputation as a long-term trading partner.

“India represents a significant long-term opportunity for New Zealand wine, and sustained cross-party commitment will be key to delivering a high-quality agreement with real commercial outcomes,” he said.

New Zealand Winegrowers views the FTA as positive news for the industry as wineries will be able to plan with confidence around a clear, decade-long tariff reduction pathway.

While it noted that current exports to India are very modest, sitting at just under NZ $300,000 (FOB) for 2025 due to the very high tariffs, “improved market access under the FTA is expected to make India increasingly attractive to New Zealand wine exporters over the coming years”.

“We value the constructive approach taken across Parliament and the shared recognition of trade as a driver of growth, jobs, and regional prosperity,” Gregan said.

title: Riverland Wine appoints new executive officer
summary:
Riverland Wine has appointed Andrew Weeks as its new executive officer, effective Monday 27 April 2026. Riverland Wine said that Weeks brings a “deep understanding of the winegrape sector shaped by a career spanning viticulture, business management, national industry advocacy, and with a personal connection to the Riverland that goes back to his childhood in Renmark”. “The Riverland has always been central to Australia’s wine industry, and I’m proud to be returning to the region in this role. Growers and winemakers here are doing it tough, and they deserve strong, practical representation. I’m looking forward to working alongside the board, members and industry partners to push for the settings that will keep this region viable and thriving for the long term,” Weeks said.

The post Riverland Wine appoints new executive officer appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Andrew Weeks will assume the position from next Monday. Image supplied

Riverland Wine has appointed Andrew Weeks as its new executive officer, effective Monday 27 April 2026.

Riverland Wine said that Weeks brings a “deep understanding of the winegrape sector shaped by a career spanning viticulture, business management, national industry advocacy, and with a personal connection to the Riverland that goes back to his childhood in Renmark”.

“The Riverland has always been central to Australia’s wine industry, and I’m proud to be returning to the region in this role. Growers and winemakers here are doing it tough, and they deserve strong, practical representation.

“I’m looking forward to working alongside the board, members and industry partners to push for the settings that will keep this region viable and thriving for the long term,” Weeks said.

As CEO of Wine Grape Growers Australia, the national advocacy body, Week’s led the organisation’s transition to become Australian Vignerons which bestowed him with experience in policy, biosecurity, and research and extension at a national level.

As senior viticulture officer at CCW Co-operative, he provided technical advice to a group that accounted for around 10% of the nations annual grape crush. He also holds a Bachelor of Agricultural Science from the University of Adelaide and has lectured in wine business at Flinders University.

Brigid Nolan, chair of Riverland Wine, said that Andrew is exactly the right person for the role right now.

“His grounding in viticulture, his experience in national advocacy, and his genuine connection to the Riverland mean he understands both the technical and commercial realities our growers and winemakers face every day.

“We look forward to having him lead our advocacy with the energy and focus this region deserves,” she said.

Riverland Wine said that Week’s will work directly with government, regulators and industry partners to ensure the Riverland’s challenges, and its essential contribution to the national wine sector are properly understood and addressed.

His appointment follows the departure of outgoing executive officer Alex Cannon-Leyson, who finished her tenure on 20 March 2026.

Riverland Wine extended thanks to Cannon-Leyson for her dedication and significant contribution to the organisation during a period of extraordinary challenge and change.

title: WISA farewells committee members and confirms 2026 leadership
summary:
The Wine Industry Suppliers Association (WISA) has acknowledged the contribution of its departing committee members while confirming its leadership team. “We formally acknowledge the individuals contributing to our management committee and the critical role volunteer leadership plays in representing and advancing the wine value chain,” WISA said. The organisation also confirmed that Amy Bishop (DW Fox Tucker) and Anna Lindsay (Rural Freight Connections) are stepping down from the committee, with Steven Scott (Vinarchy) having resigned at the end of 2025.

The post WISA farewells committee members and confirms 2026 leadership appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The 2026 WISA committee. Image supplied

The Wine Industry Suppliers Association (WISA) has acknowledged the contribution of its departing committee members while confirming its leadership team.

“We formally acknowledge the individuals contributing to our management committee and the critical role volunteer leadership plays in representing and advancing the wine value chain,” WISA said.

The organisation also confirmed that Amy Bishop (DW Fox Tucker) and Anna Lindsay (Rural Freight Connections) are stepping down from the committee, with Steven Scott (Vinarchy) having resigned at the end of 2025.

Bishop served for over six years, including as secretary and public officer, while Scott was with WISA for four years and Lindsay for one.

“We thank all for their contribution to WISA and look forward to staying connected following the strong relationships built during their time with the association. All remain WISA members and continue to support the value chain through their respective businesses,” it clarified.

WISA said it is proud to welcome Athena Stamoulos, associate from Finlaysons, who joined the committee in April following Bishop’s departure.

“Stamoulos adds critical legal and commercial capability to WISA’s committee, supporting strong governance and future-focused decision-making.”

Executive chair Liz Schoen acknowledged that the contribution of volunteer committee members remains fundamental to WISA’s ability to deliver value to members and the broader industry.

“WISA exists to champion the wine value chain, and that only happens because of the individuals who step forward to contribute their time and expertise.

“Our committee members are not removed from the challenges of the industry, they are living them in their own businesses. That makes their contribution both practical and highly relevant.

“Following the transition of WineTech and the evolution of WISA’s operating model, the role of the committee has become even more important in shaping a sustainable and commercially relevant future for supplier businesses,” she said.

WISA affirmed that it continues to focus on strengthening its role as the voice of suppliers, with initiatives spanning advocacy, professional development, industry events and collaboration across the value chain.

“We remain committed to building stronger connections between suppliers and producers, while ensuring supplier businesses maintain influence, visibility and commercial relevance within the Australian and New Zealand wine sector,” WISA explained.

The 2026 committee is as follows:

  • Liz Schoen (WISA executive chair & Liz Schoen Consulting)
  • Eric Wilkes (Australian Wine Research Institute) – deputy chair
  • Ron van Buuren (Hydra Consulting) – treasurer
  • Romina Meloni (Lallemand)
  • Tim Stead (Amorim)
  • Melissa Raymond (Garden & Field)
  • Aaron Haw (Cork Supply)
  • Athena Stamoulos (Finlaysons)
  • Adrian Fiocchini (Seguin Moreau)

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

title: WSET Aussie Diploma graduates recognised with prestigious awards
summary:
WSET celebrated the outstanding achievements of its latest cohort of Level 4 Diploma in Wines graduates at its annual graduation ceremony and prize reception at the QEII Centre in Westminster, London. A total of 714 new Diploma graduates from 2024/25 were honoured at the ceremony representing 45 countries across six continents. They join a global community of over 14,500 WSET Diploma alumni “shaping the future of the drinks industry worldwide”. Among this year's top students, Australian graduates claimed two of the most prestigious prizes: Sebastian Iannuzzi of Sydney Wine Academy took the Vintners' Cup – awarded to the highest overall performer – and Anthony Justice, also of Sydney Wine Academy, claimed the Penfolds Asia Pacific Scholarship – awarded to the top performing graduate in Asia-Pacific.

The post WSET Aussie Diploma graduates recognised with prestigious awards appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
LTR: Michelle Brampton, Sebastian Iannuzzi, Anthony Justice and Helena Edgerton. Image supplied

WSET celebrated the outstanding achievements of its latest cohort of Level 4 Diploma in Wines graduates at its annual graduation ceremony and prize reception at the QEII Centre in Westminster, London.

A total of 714 new Diploma graduates from 2024/25 were honoured at the ceremony representing 45 countries across six continents. They join a global community of over 14,500 WSET Diploma alumni “shaping the future of the drinks industry worldwide”.

The ceremony was hosted by WSET CEO Michelle Brampton, who was joined on stage by WSET’s honorary president Dr Laura Catena, managing director of Catena Zapata.

Brampton said the organisation is incredibly proud to celebrate with the class of 2024/25.

“The WSET Diploma asks a great deal of students, and this year’s graduates have risen to the challenge. Our Diploma alumni are a vibrant global community, shaping the future of the drinks industry in so many ways.

“I’d also like to personally congratulate our scholarship and prize winners, whose exceptional results, together with the stories they’ve shared about the impact WSET has had on their lives and careers, continue to inspire us all,” she said.

Catena added that graduation marks an important moment in a lifelong commitment to learning.

“The intellectual curiosity and discipline shown by this year’s graduates give me great confidence in the future of our industry.”

Among this year’s top students, Australian graduates claimed two of the most prestigious prizes: Sebastian Iannuzzi of Sydney Wine Academy took the Vintners’ Cup – awarded to the highest overall performer – and Anthony Justice, also of Sydney Wine Academy, claimed the Penfolds Asia Pacific Scholarship – awarded to the top performing graduate in Asia-Pacific.

The Vintners’ Cup, sponsored by The Worshipful Company of Vintners since 2004, is awarded annually to the graduate achieving the highest aggregate mark across all units of the WSET Level 4 Diploma in Wines.

This year’s winner Iannuzzi is production manager at Mount Eyre Vineyards and studied at the Sydney Wine Academy. His passion for wine began at his family’s Hunter Valley vineyard, where childhood weekends immersed him in viticulture and winemaking.

“Winning the Vintners’ Cup is an immense honour. My disbelief led me to reread the email several times. The further I progressed through the Diploma, the more acutely aware I became of how much I still had to learn. Surrounded by industry professionals, knowledgeable educators and exceptionally capable classmates, it’s easy to feel unlearned. The Vintners’ Cup served as a powerful reminder of how much I had achieved,” he said.

Justice, who was awarded the Penfolds Asia Pacific Scholarship, said he is delighted to have been awarded the scholarship.

“Previous study trips have been incredibly useful in gaining a much better understanding of various aspects of the industry and I am excited to put this scholarship to good use,” he said.

title: Clare Valley provides update on 2026 ‘season of resilience’ vintage
summary:
The Clare Valley Wine & Grape Association (CVWGA) is pleased to share a “confident outlook” on the 2026 vintage. CVWGA claims that early indicators are pointing to wines of “purity, balance and regional character, particularly for the Valley’s celebrated Riesling”. It claimed that the growing season presented a few challenges, with variable conditions requiring careful vineyard management and considered decision-making as harvest approached. CVWGA chair Olivia Hoffmann Barry said that the community has navigated a complex season with skill and patience. “The fruit that was harvested is showing excellent quality, with bright, well-defined flavours and a clear sense of regional character,” she said.

The post Clare Valley provides update on 2026 ‘season of resilience’ vintage appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Clare Valley Wine & Grape Association (CVWGA) is pleased to share a “confident outlook” on the 2026 vintage.

CVWGA claims that early indicators are pointing to wines of “purity, balance and regional character, particularly for the Valley’s celebrated Riesling”.

It claimed that the growing season presented a few challenges, with variable conditions requiring careful vineyard management and considered decision-making as harvest approached.

“A cool start to the season and welcome spring rainfall supported strong canopy health, while intermittent rainfall through March brought periods of pressure, placing a premium on timing, site selection and attention to detail in the vineyard.

“In well-managed sites, fruit condition remained sound, with only low levels of disease observed. In some cases, growers made disciplined choices not to harvest all fruit, ensuring that quality remained the defining priority,” CVWGA said.

CVWGA chair Olivia Hoffmann Barry said that the community has navigated a complex season with skill and patience.

“The fruit that was harvested is showing excellent quality, with bright, well-defined flavours and a clear sense of regional character,” she said.

Across the Clare Valley, early tastings and ferments are revealing “wines of clarity and precision” which CVWGA said were shaped as much by the season as by the decisions made in response to it.

Variation across subregions and picking windows is also expected to deliver a “diverse and expressive vintage, reflecting the individuality of sites and styles”.

The organisation claims that the region’s hallmark variety, Riesling, is already emerging as a standout with “vibrant citrus expression, lifted aromatics and fine, persistent acidity”.

Yields of high-quality Riesling have remained solid, CVWGA claimed, with good availability to meet the “growing global demand for the variety”.

Michael Kane of Knappstein Wines said that there is no “perfect growing season”.

“It’s our role as growers and winemakers to respond to those conditions and capture the best of what the vineyard offers. In 2026, that has resulted in wines of exceptional quality, with fine Rieslings and classic, medium-bodied reds that speak clearly of Clare Valley,” he said.

“Beyond Riesling, other white varieties such as Fiano are showing strong results, reflecting the region’s evolving varietal landscape.

“Cabernet Sauvignon is a particular highlight among the reds in the region, with dry conditions up to veraison leading to smaller berries. This,coupled with an extended ripening period (hang time) resulted in wines with intense varietal expression, depth and structure.

“Shiraz and Grenache are also showing excellent promise, contributing to a vintage defined by refined elegance and superb balance between fruit and tannin elements,” CVWGA said.

title: ASVO concludes search for new executive director
summary:
Following an “extended search for the ideal candidate”, the Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology (ASVO) has announced that Emily White starts today as its new executive director. ASVO said White brings “substantial experience in the wine and agribusiness industries” along with a “strong background of demonstrated skill in marketing and business development”. White shared that she is excited to be starting the role and is honoured to lead “an organisation with longstanding equity in the Australian wine community”. “ASVO plays a vital role in bringing together technical leadership and industry - from publishing research that drives practical improvements, to creating opportunities for members to connect and share knowledge across oenology and viticulture”.

The post ASVO concludes search for new executive director appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Emily White, the new executive director for the ASVO. Image supplied

Following an “extended search for the ideal candidate”, the Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology (ASVO) has announced that Emily White starts today as its new executive director.

ASVO said White brings “substantial experience in the wine and agribusiness industries” along with a “strong background of demonstrated skill in marketing and business development”.

White shared that she is excited to be starting the role and is honoured to lead “an organisation with longstanding equity in the Australian wine community”.

“ASVO plays a vital role in bringing together technical leadership and industry – from publishing research that drives practical improvements, to creating opportunities for members to connect and share knowledge across oenology and viticulture.

“I am particularly committed to strengthening partnerships and collaborative platforms that deliver real value to our members, while building meaningful connections across research, industry, and education. My focus will be on increasing membership value and leveraging ASVO’s trusted position to benefit both current and future generations shaping Australia’s wine sector,” she said.

Samantha Scarratt, president of ASVO, said that the organisation is delighted to have found “such a strong candidate to take the ASVO forward”.

“Emily’s strength in marketing and business development will assist the ASVO to grow its membership base and profile and will be an excellent complement to the extensive viticulture and winemaking – and recently added editorial and financial – expertise of the ASVO board of directors. Emily brings a different skillset to the ASVO, which will help lead the organisation into the future,” Scarratt said.

From today, White will collaborate closely with retiring executive director Chris Waters for the next two months as she readies herself to take over the management of ASVO services and activities.

She holds a Bachelor of Viticultural Science degree and has further studied wine marketing. ASVO said that White has a “distinguished track-record” in senior marketing and has led global as well as regional initiatives across agricultural sales, marketing, strategy and industry engagement roles.

White’s experience includes leading teams across Taltarni/Clover Hill Vineyards, Treasury Wine Estates and Accolade Wines, where she has “consistently driven brand growth, market expansion and commercial partnerships in highly competitive markets”.

title: Barossa Valley is the most affordable wine region in the world, according to new report
summary:
According to Knight Frank’s 2026 Wealth Report, the Barossa Valley is one of the most affordable wine regions by hectare globally. The firm’s global vineyard index found US $1 million can buy 18.18 hectares of land in the region, the greatest amount of land among the regions examined. The cost of land in the Barossa Valley was US $55,000 per hectare at the end of 2025 following a 10% reduction over the year, making it the most affordable wine region in the world. Stellenbosch in South Africa was the next most affordable at US $60,000 per hectare, while Italy's Piedmont (Barolo) was the most expensive at US $2.7 million per hectare.

The post Barossa Valley is the most affordable wine region in the world, according to new report appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
According to Knight Frank’s 2026 Wealth Report, the Barossa Valley is the most affordable wine region by hectare globally, despite its reputation as one of Australia’s premium wine regions.

The firm’s global vineyard index found US $1 million can buy 18.18 hectares of land in the region, the greatest amount of land among the regions examined.

The cost of land in the Barossa Valley was US $55,000 per hectare at the end of 2025 following a 10% reduction over the year, making it the most affordable wine region in the world.

Stellenbosch in South Africa was the next most affordable at US $60,000 per hectare, while Italy’s Piedmont (Barolo) was the most expensive of regions included in the index at US $2.7 million per hectare.

According to the report, despite persistent headwinds, the vineyard market is “far from being in decline”. However, it claims there are “forces reshaping the global wine production map” and “creating exciting opportunities for forward-looking winemakers and investors”.

Knight Frank Australia partner, agribusiness Jason Oster said the Barossa Valley’s recognition in the report as a wine region to watch “reaffirmed its reputation as one of the world’s great wine-producing areas”.

“With more than 175 years of winemaking history and home to some of the oldest producing vines in the world, the Barossa Valley continues to attract interest from high-net-worth-individuals seeking a combination of agricultural investment, lifestyle appeal and long-term capital preservation.

“The Barossa Valley offers a great value proposition with large vineyard scale for comparatively low capital outlay, and strong growth prospects, especially for those that are looking to make the most of the premiumisation trend.

“Australia’s winemakers continue to struggle with oversupply and the shift in consumer tastes towards lighter wines. But for those who are pursuing premiumisation strategies, focusing on higher quality regional wines, like Barossa Valley, and offering consumers unique provenance and storytelling, often through cellar-door sales, will thrive and weather the storm.

“Although there has been some downward pressure on vineyard returns, there is optimism within the market for those with premium product,” Oster said.

The research identifies five trends impacting the global wine industry, with the Barossa Valley and nearby Eden Valley named as regions to watch in Australia for the ‘trading up’ trend.

The report emphasised that “while overall global consumption of wine is falling, values are rising, with drinkers increasingly choosing higher-quality, artisanal and terroir-specific wines, rather than mass-market bottles”.

“Those vineyards with a strong narrative story focusing on provenance and craftsmanship are best placed to differentiate themselves,” the report said.

title: Barossa boutique winery wins Shiraz Producer of the Year at 2026 London Wine Competition
summary:
Family-owned Barossa Valley boutique winery Kellermeister Wines has been awarded Shiraz Producer of the Year at the global 2026 London Wine Competition. All three of Kellermeister’s flagship Shiraz wines were awarded Gold medals, with it’s the Meister Shiraz receiving a Double Gold. Winemaker Mark Pearce said it is a joy to work with his sons Hugo and Rupert, who have joined him in working at the winery for a few years now. “Together we continue to chase the dream of crafting the very best wines we can produce in this beautiful region, which I believe is the archetypal home for Shiraz in the New World - informed by the timeless wisdom of the inspiration I derive from the Rhône in France, and the unique power of our ancient vines in the Barossa,” he said.

The post Barossa boutique winery wins Shiraz Producer of the Year at 2026 London Wine Competition appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Winemaker Mark Pearce with his sons Hugo and Rupert. Image supplied

Family-owned Barossa Valley boutique winery Kellermeister Wines has been awarded Shiraz Producer of the Year at the global 2026 London Wine Competition.

All three of Kellermeister’s flagship Shiraz wines were awarded Gold medals, with it’s the Meister Shiraz receiving a Double Gold.

Winemaker Mark Pearce said it is a joy to work with his sons Hugo and Rupert, who have joined him in working at the winery for a few years now.

“Together we continue to chase the dream of crafting the very best wines we can produce in this beautiful region, which I believe is the archetypal home for Shiraz in the New World – informed by the timeless wisdom of the inspiration I derive from the Rhône in France, and the unique power of our ancient vines in the Barossa,” he said.

The London Wine Competition “is renowned for its rigorous blind-tas1ng format, judged by more than 70 of the world’s most respected wine professionals, including Masters of Wine, Master Sommeliers, senior buyers, and wine directors”.

Since Pearce joined the winery in 2010 and acquired it in 2012, the estate said it has amassed one of the most consistent award records for its Shiraz wines in the world, including Best Shiraz in the World (International Wine Competition 2012, Syrah du Monde 2019), Wine of the Year at the London Wine Competition 2019 (plus Best Wine by Country [Australia], Best Wine by Varietal [Shiraz], and Best Wine by Quality), World’s Top 5 Shiraz (Syrah du Monde 2021), World’s Top 10 Shiraz (Syrah du Monde 2023), and back-to-back Trophy Best Barossa Shiraz titles at the London International Wine Competition in 2023 and 2024.

Kellermeister’s The Meister retails for $350 per bottle, while its Wild Witch and Black Sash Shiraz retail for $88 and $92 per bottle.

title: New data shows Champagne shipments to Australia increased in 2025
summary:
Comité Champagne has revealed that more than 7.5 million bottles of Champagne were shipped to Australia in 2025, an increase of 3.5% volume from the previous year. With these figures, Australia remains the seventh largest global market for Champagne in the world. Total shipments of Champagne worldwide for 2025 was 266 million bottles, down 2% from the 271.4 million bottles distributed in 2024. International markets accounted for nearly 152 million of those bottles, whilst the French market represented 114 million bottles. “With a strong appellation and coordinated actions, we will continue to bring joy to all those around the world who love fine wines, dreams, fraternity, celebration and life,” said David Chatillon, president of the Union des Maisons de Champagne and co-president of Comité Champagne.

The post New data shows Champagne shipments to Australia increased in 2025 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Comité Champagne has revealed that more than 7.5 million bottles of Champagne were shipped to Australia in 2025, an increase of 3.5% volume from the previous year.

With these figures, Australia remains the seventh largest global market for Champagne in the world.

Total shipments of Champagne worldwide for 2025 was 266 million bottles, down 2% from the 271.4 million bottles distributed in 2024. International markets accounted for nearly 152 million of those bottles, whilst the French market represented 114 million bottles.

“With a strong appellation and coordinated actions, we will continue to bring joy to all those around the world who love fine wines, dreams, fraternity, celebration and life,” said David Chatillon, president of the Union des Maisons de Champagne and co-president of Comité Champagne.

Maxime Toubart, president of the Syndicat Général des Vignerons and co-president of the Comité Champagne, said that the French market is a showcase of its appellation that must be strengthened.

“We have all the assets we need – excellent wines, unique expertise and collective strength – to consolidate this position and promote Champagne,” Toubart said.

“The Australian market has remained resilient for Champagne, despite ongoing economic pressures and global geopolitical uncertainty,” added John Noble, director of the Champagne Bureau Australia.

“The versatility and diversity of Champagne styles ensure the wines resonate with the Australian lifestyle and across a wide range of occasions – from relaxed, everyday dining to the celebration of life’s most significant moments.”

The top ten export markets for Champagne by volume are as follows:

  1. United States of America: 26 462 000
  2. United Kingdom: 22 728 000
  3. Japan: 13 283 000
  4. Germany: 9 616 000
  5. Belgium: 7 823 000
  6. Italy: 7 785 000
  7. Australia: 7 555 000
  8. Switzerland: 5 069 000
  9. Spain: 4 004 000
  10. Canada: 2 893 000

title: Final week to have your say on the Murray-Darling Basin
summary:
The Murray-Darling Basin Authority (MDBA) has advised that this is the final week for members of the community to have their say on the future of the Murray-Darling Basin. The MDBA encourages communities to make a submission before the deadline on 1 May at 5pm. The organisation said it has already received more than 600 submissions, reflecting a “strong interest in how the Basin’s water resources should be managed over the next decade”.

The post Final week to have your say on the Murray-Darling Basin appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Murray-Darling Basin Authority (MDBA) has advised that this is the final week for members of the community to have their say on the future of the Murray-Darling Basin.

The MDBA encourages communities to make a submission before the deadline on 1 May at 5pm.

The organisation said it has already received more than 600 submissions, reflecting a “strong interest in how the Basin’s water resources should be managed over the next decade”.

MDBA chief executive Andrew McConville said the review is a once-in-a-decade chance to influence how water is shared, rivers are protected and communities are supported across the Basin.

“This review brings together the latest science, climate data and lived experience from across the Basin to assess what is working, what needs to change and how the plan must evolve.

“Our discussion paper sets out some options. They are starting points for discussion, not predetermined decisions. Competing viewpoints are not a weakness of the Basin reform process; they are its strength. Hearing those perspectives is integral to refining the plan.

“The Basin Plan was based on the best evidence available at the time. A decade on, we have more data, more experience and a changing climate, so it’s the right time to reassess how we manage our waterways and environment,” he explained.

Since consultation opened in February, the MDBA said it has engaged with more than 2,700 participants through over 200 interactions across Basin communities.

McConville claimed that the MDBA has taken a “very deliberate approach to consultation”.

“We know people are more comfortable sharing their views in smaller, local conversations rather than large town halls. That’s why our teams have been on the ground across the Basin, meeting people where they are.

“We’re hearing a wide range of views across the Basin, and that’s exactly what we need. People are challenging us, raising new ideas and testing our thinking and we welcome that. Good policy comes from honest conversations, not everyone agreeing.

“We’ll keep engaging right up until submissions close next week. Every voice matters and we want to ensure people across the Basin have the opportunity to contribute.”

The authority said that submissions will inform recommendations to Basin Governments on how the Basin Plan can be improved to respond to emerging challenges, including climate variability and changing water needs.

Submissions information can be found here. Community members who would like assistance submitting can also call the MDBA consultation hotline on 1800 230 067.

The MDBA said it has already visited 91 towns and held webinars, roundtables, and a Basin-wide Summit to gather perspectives.

title: New Zealand Winegrowers welcomes support for the India Free Trade Agreement
summary:
New Zealand Winegrowers has welcomed the announcement of Labour’s support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement between New Zealand and India, “reinforcing trade as a shared priority”. Philip Gregan, CEO of New Zealand Winegrowers, said maintenance of bipartisan alignment on trade is very positive as it gives exporters certainty and reinforces New Zealand’s reputation as a long-term trading partner. “India represents a significant long-term opportunity for New Zealand wine, and sustained cross-party commitment will be key to delivering a high-quality agreement with real commercial outcomes,” he said.

The post New Zealand Winegrowers welcomes support for the India Free Trade Agreement appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
New Zealand Winegrowers has shown support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement with India. Image courtesy New Zealand Winegrowers

New Zealand Winegrowers has welcomed the announcement of Labour’s support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement between New Zealand and India, “reinforcing trade as a shared priority”.

Philip Gregan, CEO of New Zealand Winegrowers, said maintenance of bipartisan alignment on trade is very positive as it gives exporters certainty and reinforces New Zealand’s reputation as a long-term trading partner.

“India represents a significant long-term opportunity for New Zealand wine, and sustained cross-party commitment will be key to delivering a high-quality agreement with real commercial outcomes,” he said.

New Zealand Winegrowers views the FTA as positive news for the industry as wineries will be able to plan with confidence around a clear, decade-long tariff reduction pathway.

While it noted that current exports to India are very modest, sitting at just under NZ $300,000 (FOB) for 2025 due to the very high tariffs, “improved market access under the FTA is expected to make India increasingly attractive to New Zealand wine exporters over the coming years”.

“We value the constructive approach taken across Parliament and the shared recognition of trade as a driver of growth, jobs, and regional prosperity,” Gregan said.

title: Riverland Wine appoints new executive officer
summary:
Riverland Wine has appointed Andrew Weeks as its new executive officer, effective Monday 27 April 2026. Riverland Wine said that Weeks brings a “deep understanding of the winegrape sector shaped by a career spanning viticulture, business management, national industry advocacy, and with a personal connection to the Riverland that goes back to his childhood in Renmark”. “The Riverland has always been central to Australia’s wine industry, and I’m proud to be returning to the region in this role. Growers and winemakers here are doing it tough, and they deserve strong, practical representation. I’m looking forward to working alongside the board, members and industry partners to push for the settings that will keep this region viable and thriving for the long term,” Weeks said.

The post Riverland Wine appoints new executive officer appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Andrew Weeks will assume the position from next Monday. Image supplied

Riverland Wine has appointed Andrew Weeks as its new executive officer, effective Monday 27 April 2026.

Riverland Wine said that Weeks brings a “deep understanding of the winegrape sector shaped by a career spanning viticulture, business management, national industry advocacy, and with a personal connection to the Riverland that goes back to his childhood in Renmark”.

“The Riverland has always been central to Australia’s wine industry, and I’m proud to be returning to the region in this role. Growers and winemakers here are doing it tough, and they deserve strong, practical representation.

“I’m looking forward to working alongside the board, members and industry partners to push for the settings that will keep this region viable and thriving for the long term,” Weeks said.

As CEO of Wine Grape Growers Australia, the national advocacy body, Week’s led the organisation’s transition to become Australian Vignerons which bestowed him with experience in policy, biosecurity, and research and extension at a national level.

As senior viticulture officer at CCW Co-operative, he provided technical advice to a group that accounted for around 10% of the nations annual grape crush. He also holds a Bachelor of Agricultural Science from the University of Adelaide and has lectured in wine business at Flinders University.

Brigid Nolan, chair of Riverland Wine, said that Andrew is exactly the right person for the role right now.

“His grounding in viticulture, his experience in national advocacy, and his genuine connection to the Riverland mean he understands both the technical and commercial realities our growers and winemakers face every day.

“We look forward to having him lead our advocacy with the energy and focus this region deserves,” she said.

Riverland Wine said that Week’s will work directly with government, regulators and industry partners to ensure the Riverland’s challenges, and its essential contribution to the national wine sector are properly understood and addressed.

His appointment follows the departure of outgoing executive officer Alex Cannon-Leyson, who finished her tenure on 20 March 2026.

Riverland Wine extended thanks to Cannon-Leyson for her dedication and significant contribution to the organisation during a period of extraordinary challenge and change.

title: WISA farewells committee members and confirms 2026 leadership
summary:
The Wine Industry Suppliers Association (WISA) has acknowledged the contribution of its departing committee members while confirming its leadership team. “We formally acknowledge the individuals contributing to our management committee and the critical role volunteer leadership plays in representing and advancing the wine value chain,” WISA said. The organisation also confirmed that Amy Bishop (DW Fox Tucker) and Anna Lindsay (Rural Freight Connections) are stepping down from the committee, with Steven Scott (Vinarchy) having resigned at the end of 2025.

The post WISA farewells committee members and confirms 2026 leadership appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The 2026 WISA committee. Image supplied

The Wine Industry Suppliers Association (WISA) has acknowledged the contribution of its departing committee members while confirming its leadership team.

“We formally acknowledge the individuals contributing to our management committee and the critical role volunteer leadership plays in representing and advancing the wine value chain,” WISA said.

The organisation also confirmed that Amy Bishop (DW Fox Tucker) and Anna Lindsay (Rural Freight Connections) are stepping down from the committee, with Steven Scott (Vinarchy) having resigned at the end of 2025.

Bishop served for over six years, including as secretary and public officer, while Scott was with WISA for four years and Lindsay for one.

“We thank all for their contribution to WISA and look forward to staying connected following the strong relationships built during their time with the association. All remain WISA members and continue to support the value chain through their respective businesses,” it clarified.

WISA said it is proud to welcome Athena Stamoulos, associate from Finlaysons, who joined the committee in April following Bishop’s departure.

“Stamoulos adds critical legal and commercial capability to WISA’s committee, supporting strong governance and future-focused decision-making.”

Executive chair Liz Schoen acknowledged that the contribution of volunteer committee members remains fundamental to WISA’s ability to deliver value to members and the broader industry.

“WISA exists to champion the wine value chain, and that only happens because of the individuals who step forward to contribute their time and expertise.

“Our committee members are not removed from the challenges of the industry, they are living them in their own businesses. That makes their contribution both practical and highly relevant.

“Following the transition of WineTech and the evolution of WISA’s operating model, the role of the committee has become even more important in shaping a sustainable and commercially relevant future for supplier businesses,” she said.

WISA affirmed that it continues to focus on strengthening its role as the voice of suppliers, with initiatives spanning advocacy, professional development, industry events and collaboration across the value chain.

“We remain committed to building stronger connections between suppliers and producers, while ensuring supplier businesses maintain influence, visibility and commercial relevance within the Australian and New Zealand wine sector,” WISA explained.

The 2026 committee is as follows:

  • Liz Schoen (WISA executive chair & Liz Schoen Consulting)
  • Eric Wilkes (Australian Wine Research Institute) – deputy chair
  • Ron van Buuren (Hydra Consulting) – treasurer
  • Romina Meloni (Lallemand)
  • Tim Stead (Amorim)
  • Melissa Raymond (Garden & Field)
  • Aaron Haw (Cork Supply)
  • Athena Stamoulos (Finlaysons)
  • Adrian Fiocchini (Seguin Moreau)
